volumeTitle: Stichus. Trinummus. Truculentus. Tale of a Travelling Bag. Fragments
label: Trinummus (Section 2.4.439 - Section 2.4.535)

translation.label: Scaife English translation
translation.citation: Trinummus: The Three Pieces of Money

sections.label: Section 2.4.439
sections.original: Nequam illud verbumst bene volt , nisi qui bene facit .
sections.translation: That phrase , He wishes well , is worthless , unless a person does well too .

sections.label: Section 2.4.440
sections.original: ego quoque volo esse liber : nequiquam volo ;
sections.translation: I , too , wish to be a free man ; I wish in vain . He , perhaps , might wish to become frugal ; he would wish to no purpose .

sections.label: Section 2.4.442
sections.original: Meus gnátus me ad te misit , inter te atque nos
sections.translation: My son has sent me to you to propose an alliance and bond of friendship between himself and your family . He wishes to take your sister for his wife ; and I

sections.label: Section 2.4.445
sections.original: sententia eademst et volo .
sections.translation: have the same feelings , and I desire it .

sections.label: Section 2.4.445b
sections.original: Hau nosco tuom :
sections.translation: I really don ’ t understand your ways ; amid your prosperity you are laughing at my adversity .

sections.label: Section 2.4.447
sections.original: Homo ego sum , homo tu es : ita me amabit Iuppiter ,
sections.translation: I am a man [*] : you are a man . So may Jupiter love me , I have neither come to laugh at you , nor do I think you deserving of it ! But as to what I said , my son begged me

sections.label: Section 2.4.450
sections.original: ut tuam sororem poscerem uxorem sibi .
sections.translation: to ask for your sister as his wife .

sections.label: Section 2.4.451
sections.original: Mearúm me rerum novisse aequomst ordinem .
sections.translation: It is right that I should know the state of my own circumstances . My position is not on an equal footing with yours ; seek some other alliance for yourselves .

sections.label: Section 2.4.454
sections.original: Satin tu es sanus mentis aut animi tui ,
sections.translation: Are you really sound in mind or intellect

sections.label: Section 2.4.455
sections.original: qui condicionem hanc repudies ? nam illum tibi
sections.translation: to refuse this proposal ? For I perceive that he has been found for you a very friend in need [*] .

sections.label: Section 2.4.457
sections.original: Abin hínc dierecte ?
sections.translation: Get away hence , and go hang yourself [*] .

sections.label: Section 2.4.457b
sections.original: Si hercle ire occipiam , votes .
sections.translation: Faith , if I should commence to go , you would be forbidding me [*] .

sections.label: Section 2.4.458
sections.original: Nisi quid me aliud vis , Philto , respondi tibi .
sections.translation: Unless you want me , Philto , for anything else , I have given you my answer .

sections.label: Section 2.4.459
sections.original: Benigniorem , Lesbonice , te mihi ,
sections.translation: I trust , Lesbonicus , that you

sections.label: Section 2.4.460
sections.original: quam nunc experior esse , confido fore ;
sections.translation: will one day be more obliging to me than I now find you to be . For both to act [*] unwisely and to talk unwisely , Lesbonicus , are sometimes neither of them profitable .

sections.label: Section 2.4.463
sections.original: Verum hercle hic dicit .
sections.translation: Troth , he says what ’ s true .

sections.label: Section 2.4.463b
sections.original: Oculum ego ecfodiam tibi ,
sections.translation: I will tear out your eye if you add one word .

sections.label: Section 2.4.464b
sections.original: Hercle qui dicam tamen ;
sections.translation: Troth , but I will talk ;

sections.label: Section 2.4.465
sections.original: nam si sic non licebit , luscus dixero .
sections.translation: for if I may not be allowed to do so as I am , then I will submit to be called the one-eyed man [*] .

sections.label: Section 2.4.466
sections.original: Ita tu nunc dicis , non esse aequiperabiles
sections.translation: Do you now say this , that your position and means are not on an equal footing with ours ?

sections.label: Section 2.4.468
sections.original: Dico .
sections.translation: I do say so .

sections.label: Section 2.4.468b
sections.original: Quid ? nunc si in aedem ad cenam veneris
sections.translation: Well , suppose , now , you were to come to a building to a public banquet , and a wealthy man by chance were to come there as your neighbour [*] .

sections.label: Section 2.4.470
sections.original: ( adposita cena sit , popularem quam vocant ),
sections.translation: The banquet is set on table , one that they style a public one [*] . Suppose that dainties were heaped up before him by his dependents , and suppose any-thing pleased you that was so heaped up before him , would you eat , or would you keep your place next to this wealthy man , going without your dinner ?

sections.label: Section 2.4.474
sections.original: Edim , nisi si ille votet .
sections.translation: I should eat , unless he were to forbid me doing so .

sections.label: Section 2.4.474b
sections.original: At pol ego etsi votet
sections.translation: But I , by my faith , even if he were to forbid me ,

sections.label: Section 2.4.475
sections.original: edim , atque ambabus malis expletis vorem ,
sections.translation: would eat and cram with both cheeks stuffed out ; and what pleased him , that , in especial , would I lay hold of beforehand ; nor would I yield to him one jot of my very existence . At table it befits no one to be bashful ; for there the decision [*] is about things both divine and human .

sections.label: Section 2.4.480
sections.original: Rem fabulare .
sections.translation: You say what is the fact .

sections.label: Section 2.4.480b
sections.original: Non tibi dicam dolo :
sections.translation: I will tell you without any subterfuge : I would make place for him on the highway , on the footpath , in the canvass for public honors ; but as to what concerns the stomach — by my troth , not this much ( shows the breadth of his finger-nail ) , unless he should first have thrashed me with his fists . With provisions at the present prices , a feast is a fortune without incumbrances [*] .

sections.label: Section 2.4.485
sections.original: Semper tu hoc facito , Lesbonice , cogites ,
sections.translation: Always , Lesbonicus , do you take care and think this , that that is the best , according as you yourself are the most deserving : if that you cannot attain to , at least be as near as possible to the most deserving . And now , Lesbonicus , I wish you to grant and accept these terms which I propose , and which I ask of you .

sections.label: Section 2.4.490
sections.original: di divites sunt , deos decent opulentiae
sections.translation: The Gods are rich ; wealth and station befit the Gods : but we poor mortal beings are , as it were , the salt-cellar [*] for the salt of life . The moment that we have breathed forth this , the beggar is held of equal value at Acheron [*] with the most wealthy man when dead .

sections.label: Section 2.4.495
sections.original: Mirum quin tu illo tecum divitias feras .
sections.translation: It will be a wonder if you don ’ t carry your riches there with you . When you are dead , you may , perhaps , be as good as your name imports [*] .

sections.label: Section 2.4.497
sections.original: Nunc ut scias hic factiones atque opes
sections.translation: Now , that you may understand that position and means have no place here , and that we do not undervalue your alliance ; I ask for your sister without a marriage-portion .

sections.label: Section 2.4.500
sections.original: quae res bene vortat — habeon pactam ? quid taces ?
sections.translation: May the matter turn out happily . Do I understand her to be promised ? Why are you silent ?

sections.label: Section 2.4.501
sections.original: Pro di immortales , condicionem quoius modi .
sections.translation: O immortal Gods , what a proposal !

sections.label: Section 2.4.502
sections.original: Quin fabulare di bene vortant , spondeo ?
sections.translation: Why don ’ t you say , May the Gods prosper it , I agree [*] ?

sections.label: Section 2.4.503
sections.original: Eheú ubi usus nil erat dícto , spondeo
sections.translation: Alas ! when there was no advantage in the expression , he used to say , I agree ; now , when there is advantage in it , he is not able to say so .

sections.label: Section 2.4.505
sections.original: Quom adfinitate vostra me arbitramini
sections.translation: Since you think me , Philto , worthy of an alliance with you , I return you many thanks . But though this fortune of mine has sadly diminished through my folly , I have , Philto , a piece of land near the city here ; that I will give as a portion to my sister : for , after all my follies ,

sections.label: Section 2.4.510
sections.original: solus superfit praeter vitam relicuos .
sections.translation: that alone , besides my existence , is left me .

sections.label: Section 2.4.511
sections.original: Profecto dotem nil moror .
sections.translation: Really I care nothing at all about a portion .

sections.label: Section 2.4.511b
sections.original: Certumst dare .
sections.translation: I am determined to give her one .

sections.label: Section 2.4.512
sections.original: Nostramne , ere , vis nutricem , quae nos educat ,
sections.translation: And are you ready , master , to sever that nurse from us which is supporting us ? Take care how you do it . What are we ourselves to eat in future ?

sections.label: Section 2.4.514b
sections.original: Etiam tu taces ?
sections.translation: Once more , will you hold your tongue ?

sections.label: Section 2.4.515
sections.original: tibi ego rationem reddam ?
sections.translation: Am I to be rendered accountable to you ?

sections.label: Section 2.4.515b
sections.original: Plane periimus ,
sections.translation: We are evidently done for , unless I devise something or other . Philto , I want you .

sections.label: Section 2.4.517
sections.original: Si quid vis , Stasime .
sections.translation: If you wish aught , Stasimus .

sections.label: Section 2.4.517b
sections.original: Huc cóncede aliquantum .
sections.translation: Step a little this way .

sections.label: Section 2.4.517c
sections.original: Licet .
sections.translation: By all means .

sections.label: Section 2.4.518
sections.original: Arcano tibi ego hoc dico , ne ille ex te sciat
sections.translation: I tell you this in secrecy , that neither he nor any one else may learn it of you .

sections.label: Section 2.4.519b
sections.original: Crede audacter quidlubet .
sections.translation: Trust me boldly with anything you please .

sections.label: Section 2.4.520
sections.original: Per deos atque homines dico , ne tu illunc agrum
sections.translation: By Gods and men I warn you , not to allow that piece of land ever to become yours or your son ’ s . I ’ ll tell you my reasons for this matter .

sections.label: Section 2.4.522b
sections.original: Audire edepol lubet .
sections.translation: Troth , I should like to hear them .

sections.label: Section 2.4.523
sections.original: Primum omnium olim terra cum proscinditur ,
sections.translation: First of all then , when at any time the ground is being ploughed , in every fifth furrow the oxen die .

sections.label: Section 2.4.525
sections.original: Apage , Acheruntis ostium in vostrost agro .
sections.translation: The gate of Acheron is in that land of ours .

sections.label: Section 2.4.526
sections.original: Tum vinum prius quam coctumst pendet putidum .
sections.translation: Then the grapes , before they are ripe , hang in a putrid state .

sections.label: Section 2.4.527
sections.original: Consuadet homini , credo . etsi scelestus est ,
sections.translation: He is persuading the man to something , I think . Although he is a rogue , still he is not unfaithful to me .

sections.label: Section 2.4.528b
sections.original: Audi cetera .
sections.translation: Hear the rest . Besides that , when elsewhere the harvest of wheat is most abundant ,

sections.label: Section 2.4.530
sections.original: tribus tántis illi minus redit quam opseveris .
sections.translation: there it comes up less by one-fourth than what you have sowed .

sections.label: Section 2.4.531
sections.original: Em istic oportet opseri mores malos ,
sections.translation: Ah ! bad habits ought to be sown on that spot , if in the sowing they can be killed .

sections.label: Section 2.4.533
sections.original: Neque umquam quisquamst , quoius ille ager fuit ,
sections.translation: And never is there any person to whom that piece of land belongs , but that his affairs turn out most unfortunate . Of those to whom it has belonged ,

sections.label: Section 2.4.535
sections.original: alii exulatum abierunt , alii emortui ,
sections.translation: some have gone away in banishment ; some are dead outright ; some , again , have hanged themselves . See this man , now , to whom it belongs , how he has been brought to a regular backgammoned state [*] .
